metadata.summary: Optimize sites for AI crawlers via llms.txt, Article schema, and Core Web Vitals. AI crawlers evaluate authority and factual clarity differently than search engines.

content.title: Technical SEO for AI
content.summary: Technical optimizations that make content accessible and trustworthy to AI crawlers (GPTBot, Claude-Web, PerplexityBot): creating `llms.txt` for AI crawler guidance, implementing Article schema with author credentials, clean URL structure, proper heading hierarchy, entity consistency, and explicit author authority signals. Different from traditional SEO — AI crawlers evaluate factual accuracy and authority, not just keyword density.

why.p: AI crawlers have different evaluation criteria than Google's crawler: they assess entity clarity, factual verifiability, author credentials, cross-platform consistency, and content freshness. Without `llms.txt`, AI crawlers may ingest premium/private content or ignore important pages. Without schema markup including author credentials, content appears unattributed. As AI-generated search results replace traditional SERPs, technical AI-readiness becomes a prerequisite for visibility.

list.item: Setting up a new content site or blog that needs AI crawler visibility from launch.
Auditing an existing site for AI crawlability issues (missing schema, no `llms.txt`, ambiguous entity naming).
Implementing author credential markup to improve E-E-A-T signals for AI systems.
Optimizing Core Web Vitals for AI crawler accessibility (LCP < 2.5s, CLS < 0.1).
Paywalled or premium content sites where AI crawling of content is undesirable — configure `llms.txt` to block rather than optimize.
Sites with no original content (pure aggregators) — technical optimization cannot compensate for lack of authority.
Short-lived campaign landing pages with no long-term SEO value.

section.name: llms-txt-file

rule.statement: Create /llms.txt at the domain root to guide AI crawlers: list important pages, specify citation format, declare allowed and disallowed paths, and provide AI-specific guidance not covered by robots.txt.
rule.rationale: robots.txt governs crawl access; llms.txt governs AI understanding and citation behavior. Without llms.txt, AI crawlers make assumptions about important pages and citation format that may not match your preferences.

example.title: llms.txt with crawler guidance and citation format
example.code:
# llms.txt - AI crawler guidance User-agent: * Allow: /content/ Disallow: /admin/ Disallow: /premium/
# Preferred citation format Citation-style: [Title](URL) - Author
# Key pages for entity understanding Important: /about Important: /methodology Important: /research

section.reference: Full llms.txt template with all standard sections

rule.statement: Use clean, descriptive URLs: hyphens not underscores, no session IDs, no hash routing, under 100 characters, consistent patterns across content types.
rule.rationale: AI crawlers parse URL structure to infer content hierarchy and topic. Hash-based or session-parameterized URLs are not reliably indexed. Inconsistent patterns reduce AI confidence in entity-to-URL mapping.

section.name: ai-crawler-identification
section.p: Multiple AI systems crawl the web independently. Each has its own crawler user-agent and preferences. Distinguish them to configure targeted robots.txt rules.

list.item: GPTBot: OpenAI's crawler for ChatGPT training and browsing.
Googlebot: indexes for Google SGE (AI Overviews).

rule.statement: Configure separate robots.txt rules for AI crawlers to allow public content and block premium, admin, and user-generated content paths.
rule.rationale: AI crawlers should access your authoritative content but not private or paywalled material. Blanket blocking all AI crawlers prevents visibility in AI search results; blanket allowing them risks exposure of private content.

section.name: article-schema

rule.statement: Every article must have Article schema with: author (Person type with name and credentials), datePublished, dateModified, and headline. Author schema must reference a credentials page or bio.
rule.rationale: AI systems use schema markup to attribute content to specific authors and assess credibility. Unattributed content (no author schema) is treated as lower authority than content with explicit expert attribution. dateModified helps AI assess content freshness.

example.title: Minimal Article schema with author credentials for AI citation

section.reference: Full Article schema JSON-LD template with all authority fields

rule.statement: Add FAQSchema to pages with Q&A sections. Each question-answer pair becomes a candidate for AI-generated response inclusion.
rule.rationale: AI systems extract FAQ content as direct answers to user queries. FAQSchema makes the Q&A structure machine-readable, increasing the probability that AI-generated responses cite your content for specific questions.

rule.statement: Use consistent entity naming throughout all pages: same company name, same product names, same author names across all schema and content. Never abbreviate inconsistently.
rule.rationale: AI entity recognition depends on consistent naming across sources. Inconsistent entity names (full name vs. initials vs. common abbreviation) reduce confidence in entity resolution and may result in separate entities being created for what is the same business.

rule.statement: Use a single H1 per page describing the main topic. First paragraph must define the main entity (who or what the page is about) explicitly. Use consistent entity naming in headings.
rule.rationale: AI crawlers parse heading hierarchy to build a topic model of the page. An ambiguous or missing entity definition in the opening means the AI cannot reliably attribute the content to the correct entity in its knowledge base.

rule.statement: Present statistics and data in tables or lists with source attribution. Include publication date for all time-sensitive statistics.
rule.rationale: AI systems extract factual claims for use in generated responses. Uncited statistics may be omitted or flagged as unverifiable. Dated statistics help AI assess whether the data is current enough to cite.

rule.statement: Maintain Core Web Vitals: LCP < 2.5s, INP < 200ms, CLS < 0.1. These thresholds affect both Google's crawl priority and AI Overview inclusion eligibility.
rule.rationale: Slow pages are crawled less frequently and ranked lower in AI citation priority. Pages that shift layout during load produce unreliable content extraction. Performance is a prerequisite for AI visibility, not an optimization.

rule.statement: Update time-sensitive content at least quarterly. Change dateModified in schema on every substantive update. Archive or redirect pages with statistics older than 24 months that cannot be updated.
rule.rationale: AI systems weight content freshness when selecting sources for generated responses. Outdated statistics that AI cites create trust problems for both the AI system and your brand. Stale pages that cannot be updated should be removed from AI crawler access.

rule.statement: Display author name, credentials, and a link to an author bio on every article page. Make credentials visible in the page text, not only in schema.
rule.rationale: AI systems that cannot visibly verify author credentials from page content alone may treat the content as lower authority regardless of schema markup. Schema and visible page content must both carry authority signals.

section.name: best-practices-summary
section.p: Technical SEO for AI consolidates into three key domains: crawler configuration (llms.txt, robots.txt rules, URL structure), schema markup (Article schema with author credentials, FAQSchema, entity consistency), and content structure (heading hierarchy, entity clarity, freshness signals, performance targets).

Implementation differs from traditional SEO because AI crawlers explicitly evaluate author authority, factual accuracy, and entity recognition. A page may rank well in Google without being cited in AI-generated responses if it lacks proper schema attribution or author credentials.

The 30% failure rate for sites without AI optimization comes from missing llms.txt guidance, absent author credentials, inconsistent entity naming, or poor Core Web Vitals performance. Addressing all four areas together yields the most measurable citation gains.

template.purpose: llms.txt template with crawler guidance, citation format, important page declarations
Article schema JSON-LD with author credentials, datePublished, dateModified fields
